mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage security and compliance through policy-as-code. It is particularly useful for platform engineering teams looking to enforce governance and automate policy management. Key trade-offs include the need for users to define their own policies and the potential complexity of managing these policies at scale.

steps.title: Add Kyverno Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install Kyverno from it.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Kyverno repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Kyverno
steps.description:
This step installs Kyverno in the 'kyverno' namespace using Helm, ensuring that the correct version is deployed.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kyverno

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

```

> Creates a namespace for Kyverno.
> Installs Kyverno with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify Kyverno Installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kyverno pods are running correctly in the 'kyverno' namespace.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Lists all pods in the kyverno namespace to verify the installation.

steps.title: Create Example Policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

```

> Creates a policy that requires the 'app' label on Pods.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

steps.title: Verify Policy Creation
steps.description:
This step checks if the policy was created successfully.

```bash

kubectl get clusterpolicies

```

> Lists all cluster policies to verify the new policy.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno from the cluster. Note that any policies created will be deleted, which may lead to data loss if they were enforcing configurations.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete namespace kyverno

```

> Uninstalls Kyverno from the specified namespace.
> Deletes the Kyverno namespace, removing all associated resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your policies. This step upgrades Kyverno to the latest version while ensuring the cluster remains stable.

```bash

kubectl get clusterpolicies -o yaml > backup-policies.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backs up existing cluster policies to a file.
> Upgrades Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verifies that the Kyverno pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'validation failed: missing required field'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error occurs when a resource does not meet the validation criteria set by a policy.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the resource includes all required fields as specified in the policy.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen due to misconfiguration in the Kyverno deployment or resource limits being exceeded.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' to identify the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Policy not enforcing as expected
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policy may not be correctly defined or applied to the right resources.

**Fix:**
Review the policy definition and ensure it matches the intended resources.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to create resources due to policy violation
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** A policy is preventing the creation of resources that do not comply with its rules.

**Fix:**
Modify the resource to comply with the policy or update the policy to allow the resource.

versionNotes.changes: Improved support for validating custom resource definitions (CRDs) and added new policy templates.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running with a status of 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required tools installed.
